narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in competitive fields like this. Your profile includes being in the lowest decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, an undergraduate GPA of 2.70, an LSAT score of 29, a middle-class family income, and plans to attend full-time, which is the norm for most applicants. The admissions model, trained on data from countless students' paths to the bar exam, predicted a 71.7% chance that you would fail the bar on your first try— a threshold that led to your application's rejection to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pushing toward this prediction was your third-year school decile ranking at the bottom, which stood out sharply compared to the typical student's middle-of-the-pack performance and signaled potential struggles in handling rigorous legal coursework. Your first-year decile, also at the bottom and well below average, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential red flag. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA, though below the 3.21 average, actually worked slightly in your favor by suggesting some academic resilience. Similarly, your LSAT score, lower than the usual 36.7, nudged the prediction a bit toward passing but couldn't overcome the early academic hurdles. Less impactful were your middle-income background, which had a minor push toward failure risk, and your full-time status, which helped a little since most successful students share that path. Overall, your early grades painted a picture riskier than the typical admitted student's stronger academic trajectory, tipping the scales against you. This isn't a judgment on your potential—many overcome similar starts— but a data-driven call by the model. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent academic record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and highlighting any personal growth stories in a new application. You're capable of rewriting this narrative; keep pushing, and reach out to advisors for tailored strategies. We've got your back.
